speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles April 4, 1995 Tom.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to the gathering place, and you saw your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting. They told us to sit on the cobblestones. And they were still hunting for some Jews, but the majority of us were already in this place. And later, we were loaded on trucks. I still remember a picture when mine-- he was a German, ethnic German, Mr. Steidel. He was the head of the school, public school, from before the war. I remember him by the bus, sitting on the cobblestone, him walking by on the-- on the trottoir, which is a sidewalk. And he-- like, he would demonstrate his-- that he's sorry. He was-- his head was down, he was walking. And I remember still looking at him and-- because other Poles, they'd look at us, you know, from the windows. And he was with his head down. He went through. Funny thing, that [NON-ENGLISH], the help I did get were from Germans, ethnic Germans. Not so much from Poles. And later, was an order.

speaker: interviewer
text: I must tell you this too. A Polish woman was caught, and she was included in our group. And she was screaming, [NON-ENGLISH]. She said, 'People, I'm not Jewish. Why should I suffer?'

speaker: interviewee
text: And people did come, Polish people, to verify that she's not Jewish. 'I'm not guilty.' Not guilty. We were guilty. And I remember, they took her out. They let her go. But for two minutes, she felt how hard it was to be Jewish. Later, we were loaded in, in the trucks, two trucks covered in canvas, and they moved on. In the front was a jeep with a machine gun. In the back, a jeep with a machine gun. And we realized, we are moving in the direction of Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you see, hope. What had killed the Jewish people? Hope. Maybe I will survive. And the same was in our case.

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, there were little children, old women. We can't hope. But no. Maybe they will take us to a concentration camp. Of course, on the way from Izbica to Sobibor-- 40 miles again. Like, the same distance like, to, to [? Belgius ?]. About half way was an off ramp to Osova, a concentration camp. So maybe they will take us to Osova. Everything is better than a death camp. And I remember this, this mass sigh-- [SIGHS] --when we passed the off ramp and the truck didn't turn. But the hope. And I remember the conversations. We'd talk about resistance. Voices of young people saying, let's fight back. We will die anyway. Let's fight back. And on the other side I heard voices from family men, like my father. It's no use. We will die anyway. Let's die as a family. And so the trucks come to a stop in Sobibor. Told us to go down. Stepped down. This place was all secret. That the Germans which delivered to us to Sobibor were told to move out, and they moved out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I looked around, and I find myself in front of a big, long, fence. Green fence. Why the fence was green? Because, as I find out later, it was a barbed wire fence interwoven with pine branches so nobody could look in. This the reason why it was green.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side was a big gate with a sign, black and white, SS sonderkommando, which meant SS special command. The gates opened and they told us to go in. And we went in. And this was a terrible shock for me. As a child, I was a very sensitive child. I did read a lot. My mother, the best gift was if she did bring me a book. And I imagined, I heard in the home, speaking, talking about Sobibor, so I imagined, Sobibor must be like a hell. And what a hell is, I had an idea from my Uncle Yankel. He was a storyteller. A hell is for the burned people, and tar. And there's-- and devils and this. So in this way, I imagined Sobibor. I remember once my parents were talking in the room, was in the evening, and they were talking, thinking that I don't hear anything. Surprising how much parents are not aware that kids could listen in, and they talk about Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were aware that Sobibor was a death camp. But they were discussing how they're killing us. Is it gas? Is it electricity? Is it steam? In my heart, it just pop. And now I'm in Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: But instead, seeing this imaginative hell I had my mind, tar, people, it was unbelievable clean. It was neat. It was like at a summer place visit here in America. Quiet, green. We were standing in a black paved road. Along sides were flowers, flower beds. In the right side was a tidy train station with a signpost to Warsaw, to Budapest. On the left side were little houses, Tyrolean type villas with fancy names like [? Svalbenest ?], Swallow's Nest, or [? Gotosthimet, ?] God's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought, no. It can't be here. It can't be a death-- it doesn't look like. And I still have frozen in my mind the two-- [INAUDIBLE] the clouds in the middle of the [? bills ?] because Sobibor was in a forest, you know? Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But farther on, and about 200 yards, there was another fence, similar to this fence in the front, also green. Behind this fence I've seen a fire. I didn't see what was burning, but it was a fire. And the smell. And I realized, this is the end of the road. Now, Frenzel, the SS man-- I find out that his name was Frenzel, and this was later. I heard it in my interview after the war, a few years ago in Germany. Frenzel ordered, women and children on one side. Men on the other side. I was, in that year, in that time, 15 years old. This is a age, it could go both ways. But I was with my mother. So I kissed my mother goodbye. And I said something, which I can't forgive myself till now. You are a psychologist. You should-- maybe-- maybe you will understand. Many times I think I would go to a psychiatrist to resolve this, and I didn't go till now. And maybe, probably I will die and I wouldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I said was this. You see, a day before they took us to Sobibor I was thirsty. I told my mommy, Mommy, could I have a glass of milk? And she said, yes. Open the icebox. In Poland at that time, they had wooden boxes with ice, with pieces of ice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I opened the ice box, took the milk, and start to drink. And she said, oh, stop it. Leave something for tomorrow. Tomorrow is another day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I said now to my mother is, you see, Mommy? You said today is another day. You didn't let me drink the milk. And she said, is that's all you have to say? And this way we, parted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I left to-- to-- to my father. And my ten year old brother left my mother. We passed actually, we crossed that way. And when the division was conducted, the order was in the [NON-ENGLISH] for the women and children to go in the direction where the fence was. And they left. And this was the last time I've seen them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later, we men were standing. And Frenzel turned to us and said, who is a carpenter? Who is a tailor? Who is a mechanic? And people know this was their last chance to stay alive, picked up their hands. I'm a mechanic. Me, 15 years old, what could I be? 15. So I go to school. But inside I begged-- at that time, I still believed in God-- and begged to God, God, help me. Please, let this stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pray to this German, please, take me. And I still believe, I strongly believe. You know, you're aware, besides vocal communication, there's something which people could transmit like a radio wave. I still believed that some way my strong will reached him. Because at a point when he passed me, when our eyes met, and inside that I said to me, you must take me. He stopped, looked at me, and said, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was my shoe person. Come out, you little one. You will be my shoeshine boy. And I stepped out and was attached to the group of about 40 people. In this way, I was included in the working force of Sobibor. The rest, we are told-- my father included, my brother-- my brother go with my mother. My father, who did go, they were ordered in the direction where the fire was, where I find out later, the gas chamber were. This way I started to work in Sobibor. To tell you what I have done in Sobibor, I must tell you the working of Sobibor, the mechanics. You see, I just finished my manuscript. The title is But [INAUDIBLE], I put in three eventually titles. And one, Of course, I do feel guilty. Maybe the editor for Northwest University will consider this as a good title, because in a way, I feel guilty. What was-- I will explain-- what was the mechanics. I tell you the history of a Dutch transport. A Dutch transport usually arrived in the morning, sometime in the nighttime. They arrived about two thousand people. About 10 wagons were pushed in inside the camp. The rest waited outside. The people were told to step down. The Polish people were aware that it's a death camp, but the Dutch didn't. They went down. There was a group of us prisoners called bahnhof kommando. We helped them with the heavy luggage. And us, you have seen in this film maybe, Some did even want to tip us for the help, for their heavy luggage. So unaware. When they went down they were told to leave the heavy luggage and march on. The baggage will be delivered later. And they marched on. They went to, to a big, huge barrack. A German-- a barrack for German-- military barrack for German horses. Was long with the entrance gate and the exit gate, the way you have it seen in the museum or maybe the model I made.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over there, why was this barrack built? It was built special, as I understood, for the woman. Because people departed with the valises. But the woman usually stick to the handbags. They have the most precious things there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this place probably designed so the handbags could be taken away. Because when the woman passed the length of the barn, they were told to leave the handbags. If they didn't want to, they were beaten by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the first time I noticed in the eyes of the women, some kind of suspicion. Why they are taking this away? But it didn't take long. They left by the exit gate, and they were let to a yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, a Nazi in a white uniform had a speech to them. He kindly apologized for the long trip from Holland. But unfortunately, before they get their apartments, because of sanitary conditions, they must have a shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it will take a little bit longer till they will relax. Now, he said, if they want, here on the table are post cards. They could pick up post cards to send to their family that they arrived in a beautiful place. Sobibor was a nice place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people, you know, like, [INAUDIBLE] grabbed the post cards. And there wasn't much space to write, but we are here. It looks nice. We are promised apartment. Of course, if something was written bad, the Germans didn't deliver. And the Germans collect it and they mailed it, mailed it to the cards to Holland. As a matter of fact, I've seen the card after the war in Holland. Yeah. And-- [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the cards were collected. They were ordered to undress, to tie the shoes, they shouldn't be lost, and to march. On they marched through a hallway, about 200 yards long. About five-- I would say, six feed wide between barbed wires.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were marched-- they were marched naked to-- the men went earlier, straight to the gas chambers. The women were-- on the end of this corridor were barracks, and they were directed to the barrack for the hair was shorn. And later, when they were-- the hair was shorn, they went back to the main corridor and met with the men in the gas chambers. In there, they were locked. I am sure the deception was perfect. I'm sure that when they were inside and when they noticed through the shower heads gas had come instead of water, I'm sure that they probably thought it was some kind of mechanical mistake, so was the deception. But it's too late. Doors were locked, and took-- I still remember the terrible screams. Lasts about 15 minutes, slowly subsiding. And later was quiet. And they were later taken out to the cremation pits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was the mechanics. This was the way they were killed. Now, what did we do? Now we come in. The job of the prisoners mainly was sorting.

speaker: interviewee
text: After they were killed, there were heaps of clothing, mountains. And there were about 150, 200 people standing around, picked up shirts to shirts, jackets to jackets, pants to pants. Another group did search the paints, money to money, rings to rings, watch to watches. Everything was sorted like an assembly-line factory. [INAUDIBLE]. When the blankets, the blanket with money was full, it was taken by a worker to the [NON-ENGLISH] magazine, the store room of the valuables. And when the shoes-- was a full blanket of shoes, the shoes was taken to the shoe store room. And this was our job The terror was terrible. The food was the same as in the [INAUDIBLE] concentration camp. A little bit coffee in the morning. And lunchtime, a little-- a pot with soup. And in the evening, also a little bit coffee with a little piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, as some people who have seen the film they say-- I heard it in Poland-- it's a Hollywood movie. Why? And one, one fellow from Auschwitz told me, this is a Hollywood movie. I know. I was five years in Auschwitz and I've seen how prisoner from concentration camps look like, bones, skin and bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know that Sobibor was a death camp. The food rations were the same, but we were sorting. When a transport from Holland arrived, the people didn't know where they were coming, where they are going. And the Juderat, the Jewish elders in Holland, supplied them with three packets of food stuff, which we later, we sorted it. And while we were sorting it, we eat. It was forbidden but two, three Germans, they can't watch us. They turn there head there, we did eat. So actually, it was a truthful film. And so life going-- was going [INAUDIBLE] Sobibor. Many times I woke up-- I remember I was sleeping on the third floor, the third floor on the bunk, far away from the entrance door. This was-- I must have a tremendous instinct, because the first thing I have done, I run and far away, not to be in the front. I've done a few things which maybe helped me to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, but many times, I would wake up in the morning, looked outside, and I've seen-- because I was on the third bunk-- I've seen far away, the barbed wires. The little farms and smoke coming out. And I thought, no. This must be a dream. How could they burn and gas people? It must be a terrible dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interesting that whenever I sleep in Santa Barbara, I'd wake up in the morning and look outside, seeing the ocean, an opposite thought would come. Maybe this is a dream. Although I know, 99.9%, it's not a dream, but still.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working there. I always did look for a job I shouldn't be visible to the Nazis. So it won't take too long to talk, but my last job, my longest job in Sobibor, was burning documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called me fireman in Sobibor. I had a building. I had a big incinerator inside. And when the people, which were sorting, emptied the pockets from the pants and jackets, from the documents, they brought the papers to me in blankets, and I burned them. Sometimes they sneak in a blanket of money because didn't want to give it to the Germans. So I was burn it the first round. Otherwise, usually they threw the trash, the papers, in a special place, and I burned them at comes. But when a guy would come with a blanket of money, I put it immediately in the fire. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you couldn't use this money to bribe people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sobibor, money had no-- had no-- they used to-- I used to-- I-- we-- I used some money for some purpose. You see, I will, I will tell you because it's an interesting insight in Sobibor. When a Dutch transport, or German, or a French transport, they didn't know they're going to die. But a Polish transport, they were aware. But as I said, still hope is with us to the last minute. There's a Polish book, which meant--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hope dies last.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dies last. And this is a true book. So people, knowing they were going-- nude, undressed, going to death, they still had, in there hand, some money. Maybe on the way they will-- still hope. But half way in this, in this corridor, they realized, probably, that they are going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's happened? I will come to it. One day Frenzel called me. Eh, Kleine, come. Little one. He took me from the-- probably they need people-- took me from my fireplace, from the place I was working. And they give me a rake, and told me to go and clean up the hallway, this corridor, because the next 500 people were coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was raking. You know, you must do everything perfect because the terror was so terrible. You rake, and the Germans still see like, a piece of paper, they would shot you for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very careful, but I've seen something when I was raking the sand. Something was going between the teeth of the rake. And when I bent down-- little piece of paper-- I picked up some paper. And I look close, and it was money. So if we talk about the resistance. In our 20th century, we talk about a resistance, we associate resistance with guns. But this was a spiritual resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Imagine going to do death and still sabotaging, knowing they will die, taking the time and ripped the paper. And so little pieces should be of no use to the Germans. This is really heroism.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I was-- I've seen many cases, different cases of such act. And so I've seen different Sobibor. Of course, as I said, I could talk day and night, but we must move on. So we start to organize. There were a few cases where a bigger group of people tried to escape. And it did partially-- it was [INAUDIBLE] came partially it wasn't. they were caught. Different, terrible situations. Other move on direct to the revolt. How many Jews lived in Sobibor?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I want to say. The working force was about 650 people. But, you see, what's the difference between a death camp and a concentration camp, actual all the camps, the Nazi camps, were designed to kill people. But when in a concentration camps, even the worst of them, Auschwitz, about 20-25% had a chance to survive and did. So the death camps, it was zero because, there were no selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people will ask me, if there are no selections, how come they selected you, me? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the terror was so terrible that after about two, three months, from the 650 people it had dropped about 400. They killed us for every little thing we were shot. You don't go straight, you don't sing. Because we were ordered to sing when we are marching, German songs, different kinds. We were drilled like the SS we were drilled because we were different exercise anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, where I am? The revolt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know at the revolt but I-- So we had about 650 people so have-- this is it, how was I selected? Well how come I was selected when there's no selection? Because, as I said, after a while, the workers amount dropped and they needed more people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once in a while, they did pick out from a transport. A day before my transport arrived, the Nazis killed 72 Dutch Jews, so they did need to replace. So I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So about 650 workers. We realized that there's no way out from Sobibor. There were tries before to organize [? SK ?] but we were betrayed. So this time our organization was limited to only a few people, less than 10%. Much less, because about 30 people were involved in the organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this maybe is the reason for the success. And maybe still, we wouldn't escape. The leader of the underground organization was Leon Feldhendler from a city [? Zulkeflee ?] in Poland. Maybe still we would, at the end, we'll be able to talk about the revolt and we would be killed anyway. But in 1943, in the end, the Germans were liquidating the ghettos in the east because the Red Army was pushing. At that time, they liquidated a ghetto of Minsk, and they brought the Minsk Jews to Sobibor. Now, when a transport of Minsk Jews arrived, there were so many people that the Nazis didn't have enough-- they picked up some people to help us. And they were looking for young people. They picked up about 70 young people, not realizing that most of them are former Russian POWs. You see, when the Nazis discovered a Jew as a prisoner-- in a prisoner of war camps, they immediately took him out. So they took them out and put them in the, in the Minsk ghetto. And when the Minsk ghetto was liquidated, they brought them to Sobibor. And when in Sobibor, young people were selected. This Frenzel picked up the young people, and he picked up quite a few former officers from the Soviet Army. And Feldhendler immediately recognized the possibilities. And we get-- there was a man called Sasha Peshkosky. He was a lieutenant in the Soviet Army, who did look very healthy. There was something in him, and he was big [INAUDIBLE]. He had contact with them. And finally because of him, because of his knowledge, the organization took off. And we decided to escape October 13, 1943. The plan was very simple. It was divided in two phases. The first phase, which should last from 4:00 to 5:00, was the killing of the Nazis. Killing them quietly, without arousing any suspicion, with knives and axes. The second phase was that after killing, when we assemble for the roll call, we should explode and have an open revolt and use the guns we'd needed to get from the killed Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How to kill the Germans? From now, from the perspective, I think it was very easy. You see, the Germans depend on the guards on the towers, on the Ukrainian guards. They, themselves, were the staff.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sorting place, where we were sorting, there was always a German, [? Wolf ?] was his name, who didn't supervise we shouldn't escape, he supervised we should walk. And so on in other places too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but they weren't obligated, like the guards in the towers to stay on the spot. They could go grab a cup of coffee, they could go away. So if he killed one Nazi supervising the, the sorting of the shoes, then another Nazi who passed by, he didn't see him, he wasn't alarmed. So and everything was running smooth with the Kapos, the Kapos that run everything. The regular flow was like that. Five would let you finish the work, the Kapo whistled the whistle. And we assembled in groups, and we would march to the main yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, we go to the kitchen to pick up our food. And 5:30, a half an hour later, after they eat, was the roll call. That time-- this was the dangerous time-- because at that point in the role call, all Germans must come assemble and they count us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, from 4:00 to 5:00, we could kill them very careful.
